name: Santuari de Cura Loop via Porreres and Petra
name_original: Subida al Santuario de Cura — circular por Porreres y Petra
description: This moderate 50.1-mile road cycling route ascends to the Sanctuary of Cura, offering panoramic island views from its summit.
region_intro: Road cycling around Llucmajor, Mallorca, features diverse terrain, including extensive flat plains, gently rolling hills, and challenging ascents. The region is characterized by picturesque Mallorcan countryside with windmills and almond trees, alongside coastal routes offering views of the Mediterranean Sea. Cyclists can experience both rural charm and dramatic sea cliffs, with varied elevation profiles suitable for different fitness levels. Best road cycling routes around Llucmajor The most popular road cycling route is Llucmajor Cycling Route, a 42.2 miles (67.9 km) trail that takes 2 hours 52 minutes to complete. This route offers diverse terrain, including coastal sections and inland roads. Another top favourite among local road cyclists is Santuari de Cura Loop via Porreres and Petra, a moderate 50.1 miles (80.6 km) path. This route includes significant elevation gains, leading to panoramic views across Mallorca. Local road cyclists also love the Road to S’Estanyol – Arenal–Llucmajor Cycle Path loop from s'Arenal, a 71.7 miles (115.4 km) trail leading through coastal areas and the Arenal–Llucmajor Cycle Path, often completed in about 4 hours 41 minutes. Road cycling around Llucmajor is defined by flat plains, rolling hills, and coastal paths. The network offers options for different ability levels, from easy rides to more challenging ascents. The routes in Llucmajor are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 4.7 stars from over 400 reviews. More than 11,000 road cyclists have used komoot to explore Llucmajor's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: What kind of terrain can I expect when road cycling in Llucmajor?
A: Llucmajor offers a diverse range of terrain, from extensive flat plains ideal for warm-up rides to gently rolling hills and challenging ascents. You'll find picturesque Mallorcan countryside with windmills and almond trees, as well as stunning coastal routes with views of the Mediterranean Sea and dramatic sea cliffs.

Q: How many road cycling routes are available around Llucmajor?
A: There are over 90 road cycling routes around Llucmajor, offering a wide variety of distances and elevation profiles. This includes options for all skill levels, from easy rides to more challenging ascents.

Q: Are there road cycling routes suitable for beginners in Llucmajor?
A: Yes, Llucmajor has numerous routes suitable for beginners. Approximately 24 routes are classified as easy, featuring flatter sections and less strenuous climbs. An example is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/32730221" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Windmill Near Llucmajor – Arenal–Llucmajor Cycle Path loop from Cala Blava&lt;/a&gt;, which is an easy 28.6 km ride.

Q: Are there challenging road cycling routes for experienced riders?
A: Absolutely. For experienced riders seeking a challenge, Llucmajor offers routes with significant elevation gains and panoramic views. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1002951344/santuari-de-cura-loop-via-porreres-and-petra" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Santuari de Cura Loop via Porreres and Petra&lt;/a&gt; is a moderate 80.6 km route with nearly 900 meters of elevation gain, leading to unforgettable vistas across Mallorca.

Q: What scenic viewpoints or landmarks can I see along road cycling routes in Llucmajor?
A: Many routes offer breathtaking views. The ascent to &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/79390" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Sanctuary of Cura, Puig de Randa&lt;/a&gt; provides panoramic vistas across the island. Coastal routes often pass by the Cliffs and Lighthouse of Cap Blanc, Palma Bay, and offer glimpses of Cabrera island. You can also find routes near beautiful beaches like &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/3870889" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Cala Pi Beach&lt;/a&gt; and &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/127424" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Es Trenc Beach&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: Are there any cultural or historical sites accessible by road bike?
A: Yes, you can incorporate cultural stops into your rides. Llucmajor town itself features the 18th-century St. Miguel's Church. Nearby Algaida is known for its history and the Gordiola Glass Factory. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/79390" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Sanctuary of Cura, Puig de Randa&lt;/a&gt; is a significant religious site with historical importance and stunning views.

Q: What do other road cyclists enjoy most about cycling in Llucmajor?
A: Road cycling in Llucmajor is highly rated by the komoot community, with an average score of 4.7 stars from over 400 reviews. Cyclists often praise the well-maintained roads, the diverse landscapes ranging from flat plains to challenging climbs, and the scenic coastal and rural paths that offer both natural beauty and cultural insights.

Q: Are there road cycling routes with cafe stops or places to rest?
A: Yes, many routes wind through or near charming towns and villages like Llucmajor and Algaida, which offer cafés and restaurants perfect for a break. The rural routes also pass by traditional fincas, providing scenic spots to pause and enjoy the surroundings.

Q: Can I find circular road cycling routes in Llucmajor?
A: Yes, Llucmajor offers many circular road cycling routes. For instance, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/13758382" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;View of Palma Bay – Cala Pi loop from Torre de Cala Pi&lt;/a&gt; is a popular 71.2 km circular route that offers coastal views.

Q: What is the best time of year for road cycling in Llucmajor?
A: Mallorca generally offers excellent cycling conditions for much of the year. Spring and autumn are particularly popular due to mild temperatures and less crowded roads. Summers can be hot, especially inland, but coastal routes might offer a refreshing breeze. Winters are typically mild, making it a good destination for off-season training.

Q: Are there coastal road cycling routes with sea views?
A: Absolutely. Llucmajor's coastal areas provide stunning routes with sea views. You can cycle along paths that pass by the Cliffs and Lighthouse of Cap Blanc, Palma Bay, Cala Pi, S'Estanyol, and Sa Rápita, often with glimpses of Cabrera island in the distance. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1005264900/llucmajor-cycling-route" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Llucmajor Cycling Route&lt;/a&gt; includes diverse terrain, with notable coastal sections.

Q: Is Llucmajor suitable for family-friendly road cycling?
A: While many routes are designed for road cyclists, there are options that can be adapted for families, especially the flatter sections and quieter back roads. Routes near Palma Aquarium or those that stick to the extensive flat plains are generally more suitable for a family outing. Always check the route's difficulty and elevation profile to ensure it matches your family's capabilities.

Q: What are the parking options for cyclists in Llucmajor?
A: Llucmajor town offers various parking options, and many routes conveniently start and finish at accessible points, including areas near Palma Aquarium. When planning your ride, it's advisable to check specific starting points for available parking facilities.
tips: Just a few years ago, Llucmajor and Porreres were connected by a narrow side road that meandered idyllically through the landscape and had a lot of altitude to offer. Today the towns are connected by a very well developed country road, the width of which, however, in no way reflects the traffic. You can still cycle here in pairs. Due to the expansion, a few meters of altitude have been lost, although the attraction still lies in the profile of the road. It's not flat here! The apex is reached at kilometer stone 8.
Relatively quiet road between Petra and Felanitx
People simply meet in Petra. Lots of like-minded people gather to eat and drink on the market square. I've met Marcel Wüst here more than once.

In the 19th century grain mill there is a museum with archaeological finds from nearby Son Fornés.
https://de.wikipedia.org/wiki/Son_Fornés

For many, the ascent to Cura Monastery only begins in Randa. That's not quite right. Because from Montuïri to Rand you have to cover almost 200 meters in altitude. And you usually have the wind against you. So it's no wonder that you get a little desperate here when things just don't go well. In the opposite direction, of course, it looks different. You literally fly there.

Well-known excursion and hiking destination, accordingly heavily frequented. In addition to the well-frequented gastronomy, there is also a rest area with tables and benches for a less expensive rest. It is much quieter in the monastery chapel than in the back part of the monastery with the gastronomy and the rest area.

The formation of Mount Randa:
An old legend tells that a long time ago a giant crossed the Mediterranean Sea from Algiers. He carried a basket full of stones and earth on his head, and he had tied a ship under his feet so that he would not sink. During a storm off Cabrera, both ships drifted apart and, in order not to lose his balance, he braced himself with his stick on the shore of Cala Pi. But it didn't help, he fell anyway and the contents of his basket spilled out onto the plains of Mallorca. This is where Randa Mountain came from. And from a drop of sweat that hit the ground next to the mountain, the Na Tomasa spring was born.
But that's just a legend, the real story is much scarier:
Mount Randa is hollow inside and supported by four golden pillars. Should the pillars break, not only the mountain but all of Mallorca will collapse. And not only that, "The waves of the sea will crash over Mallorca, and that will be the end of the world," says the phophecy. The Mallorcans say that three of the pillars are already broken and the fourth is damaged.

faqs.question: How long does it typically take to complete this circular road cycling route?
faqs.answer: This road cycling route, covering approximately 80.6 km, typically takes around 3 hours and 46 minutes to complete. This duration is for the cycling itself, so factor in extra time for stops at viewpoints or villages like Porreres and Petra.

faqs.question: What is the difficulty level of this ride to the Sanctuary of Cura?
faqs.answer: The Ascent to the Sanctuary of Cura — circular via Porreres and Petra is rated as moderate for road cycling. While it requires good fitness, the climb to Cura itself has an average gradient of about 5.2% over 4.52 km, making it accessible to a wide range of fitness levels without overly suffocating slopes.

faqs.question: What is the terrain and road surface like on this route?
faqs.answer: The route generally features mostly well-paved surfaces, typical for road cycling in Mallorca. The climb to Santuari de Cura includes a series of switchbacks and is well-maintained. However, be aware that the road surface on the descent from Cura might not be as smooth in some sections. You'll also experience quiet, flat back roads in the middle of the island.

faqs.question: Where can I park to start this circular route?
faqs.answer: While the route is circular, you can typically find parking in the towns along the way, such as Porreres or Petra, to begin your ride. These towns offer convenient starting points for exploring the loop.

faqs.question: What kind of views can I expect on this ride?
faqs.answer: From the summit of the Sanctuary of Cura, Puig de Randa, you'll be rewarded with panoramic views of the entire island, including the Bay of Palma and the Serra de Tramuntana mountain range. The route also passes through charming villages and showcases the scenic Pla de Mallorca region.

faqs.question: Are there any interesting stops or landmarks along the way?
faqs.answer: Yes, the route takes you through picturesque towns and past several points of interest. You'll cycle through the Felanitx Old Town, and the historic Plaça Ramon Llull, Petra. The Son Fornés Archaeological Museum and Windmill is another notable stop, and of course, the historic Sanctuary of Cura itself.

faqs.question: What is the best time of year to cycle this route?
faqs.answer: The best time to cycle this route is typically during the spring (March to May) and autumn (September to November) months. The weather is generally milder and more pleasant for cycling, avoiding the intense heat of summer and the cooler, potentially wetter winter months.

faqs.question: Is this road cycling route suitable for families or beginners?
faqs.answer: While the overall route is rated moderate, the climb to Cura is described as accessible to a wide range of fitness levels. If you are a beginner or cycling with family, ensure everyone has good fitness for the distance and elevation gain. The quiet back roads are generally pleasant, but the ascent requires some effort.

faqs.question: Is this road cycling route dog-friendly?
faqs.answer: This is a road cycling route, and while dogs are generally not suitable companions for road cycling, you might find opportunities for short walks with a leashed dog in the villages along the way. However, the primary activity is cycling, which is not dog-friendly.

faqs.question: Do I need any permits or pay entrance fees for this route?
faqs.answer: No, you do not need any special permits or pay entrance fees to cycle this route. The roads are publicly accessible. There might be a small fee to enter certain attractions or museums if you choose to visit them, but not for the cycling itself.

faqs.question: Are there places to eat or drink along the route?
faqs.answer: Yes, the route passes through several towns, including Porreres and Petra, where you will find cafes, restaurants, and shops to refuel. There are also facilities at the Sanctuary of Cura itself, which often includes a restaurant or cafe.

faqs.question: Does this route intersect other well-known cycling paths?
faqs.answer: Yes, this route incorporates sections of other established cycling paths. Notably, it covers parts of the [CIMA IB02] Santuari de Cura * Montuiri and [CIMA IB02] Santuari de Cura * Randa routes, which are popular climbs in the region.
